did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (GoogLeNet-InceptionV3, EfficientNetV2B3, and Swin Transformer) and explainable AI (Grad-CAM) to classify skin cancer using dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results benchmarking three deep learning models (GoogLeNet-InceptionV3, EfficientNetV2B3, and Swin Transformer) on the ISIC archive dataset. It includes a methodology section, reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 93.8% test accuracy for EfficientNetV2B3), and discusses new findings, classifying it as a research paper despite being published in a conference proceedings (ICCIT).

title: DermaXNet: Benchmarking Explainable Deep Learning Models for Skin Cancer Diagnosis Using Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study benchmarks three deep learning models—GoogLeNet-InceptionV3, EfficientNetV2B3, and Swin Transformer—for skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive. To address class imbalance, the researchers applied data augmentation and split the dataset into training, validation, and test sets in a 70:15:15 ratio. The models were evaluated using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. EfficientNetV2B3 achieved the best performance with 93.8% test accuracy and an F1 score greater than 0.93, outperforming the Swin Transformer and GoogLeNet-InceptionV3. Additionally, Grad-CAM was integrated to provide clinically interpretable visual explanations of class-discriminative lesion areas. The results demonstrate that EfficientNetV2B3 offers strong potential for reliable, explainable AI-assisted skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary diagnostic labels in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. The study included 8,061 benign cases in the final collection of 10,015 images.

One of the three primary diagnostic labels in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. The study included 1,824 malignant cases. The paper notes that melanoma is a dangerous form of malignant skin cancer, and basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma are non-malignant (benign) forms, though the model classified based on the broader 'Malignant' vs 'Benign' vs 'Indeterminate' categories.

One of the three primary diagnostic labels in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. The study included 130 indeterminate instances. The EfficientNetV2B3 model was noted for its efficacy in accurately identifying these lesions.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (Benign, Malignant, Indeterminate)
all_task_types.details: The paper benchmarks deep learning models for skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images. The task involves categorizing lesions into three discrete classes: Benign, Malignant, and Indeterminate, based on the HAM10000 dataset. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images obtained from the ISIC Archive. The study used a filtered subset of the original dataset, retaining only the picture ID (ISIC ID) and primary diagnosis metadata.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (GoogLeNet-InceptionV3, EfficientNetV2B3, Swin Transformer) after data augmentation and preprocessing. Split into 70% of the dataset.
use.validation: Used for validation. Split into 15% of the dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing and final evaluation. Split into 15% of the dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was compared using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

HAM10000.notes: All models reached high AUC values ranging from 0.97 to 1.00. EfficientNetV2B3 attained the highest ability to distinguish between malignant lesions and benign or indeterminate ones.

architecture_used.main_model: Three models were benchmarked: GoogLeNet (Inception V3), EfficientNetV2B3, and Swin Transformer (Swin-Tiny).
architecture_used.pretraining: All three models were pretrained on the ImageNet dataset.

GoogLeNet_InceptionV3.backbone: Pretrained InceptionV3 backbone with base layers frozen during initial training.
GoogLeNet_InceptionV3.classification_head: Global Average Pooling followed by three Dense layers (512, 256, 128 units) with GELU activation, Batch Normalization, and Dropout layers (rates 0.5, 0.3, 0.2 respectively), concluding with a softmax layer for three-class categorization.

EfficientNetV2B3.backbone: Pretrained EfficientNetV2B3 backbone with base layers initially frozen.
EfficientNetV2B3.classification_head: Final softmax output layer, a Dense layer with 512 units (GELU activation, Dropout rate 0.4), a Dense layer with 128 units (GELU activation, Dropout rate 0.3), Global Average Pooling, and Batch Normalization.

Swin_Transformer.classification_head: Custom head consisting of a Dropout layer (0.4) followed by a fully connected softmax output layer for three classes.

training_details.hardware: 16 GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4050 GPU (6 GB VRAM)

EfficientNetV2B3.class_weights: Applied using inverse class frequency strategy

preprocessing_techniques: Metadata filtering: Kept only picture ID (ISIC ID) and primary diagnosis from the original metadata file.
Image selection: Filtered dataset to 10,015 dermoscopic images with unique image IDs and primary diagnosis.
Resizing: Images resized to 299x299 pixels for Inception V3, 300x300 pixels for EfficientNetV2B3, and 224x224 pixels for Swin Transformer.
Normalization: Pixel values normalized to the [0,1] range using a scale ratio of 1/255.0.
Color format maintenance: All images maintained in RGB format.
Stratified split: Dataset split into training, validation, and test sets (70:15:15 ratio) to maintain class proportions.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to replicate orientation variations with a frequency (probability) of 0.5.
Applied for rotational symmetry with rotation transforms carried out within 120 degrees.
Applied random changes to brightness and contrast levels up to 20% to mimic lighting changes.
Images were cropped and resized within a range of 90 to 110 percent to introduce zoom effects.
Images were shifted along the X-axis and Y-axis by the maximum possible width and height, but not more than 10 percent.
To address class imbalance, 4,000 data samples were created for the Indeterminate class, 3,000 for the Malignant class, and 400 for the Benign class.
A class-weighting strategy involving inverse class frequency was employed during training to give higher penalties to the misclassification of minority classes.

conclusions: EfficientNetV2B3 achieved the best performance among the tested models, with 93.8% test accuracy, 93.68% validation accuracy, and an F1 score greater than 0.93.
The Swin Transformer demonstrated strong performance with 90.67% test accuracy and an F1 score between 0.90 and 0.92.
GoogLeNet-InceptionV3 showed lower performance compared to the other models, achieving 88.17% test accuracy and 83.38% validation accuracy.
All three models achieved high ROC-AUC values ranging from 0.97 to 1.00, with EfficientNetV2B3 attaining the highest ability to distinguish between malignant lesions and benign or indeterminate ones.
Grad-CAM provided clinically interpretable visual explanations by highlighting class-discriminative lesion areas, enhancing model transparency and trust in AI-assisted decisions.
Data augmentation and class-weighting strategies were effective in addressing class imbalance, particularly for the underrepresented Indeterminate and Malignant classes.

The study confirms the efficacy of deep learning models, particularly EfficientNetV2B3, for accurate skin lesion classification and provides a framework for reliable, explainable AI-assisted skin cancer diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (HAM10000 from the ISIC archive) without external validation, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance (8,061 benign vs. 1,824 malignant vs. 130 indeterminate), which was addressed via data augmentation and class weighting rather than solely through natural distribution.
Clinical validation by dermatologists was not performed; interpretability was assessed only via Grad-CAM heatmaps, leaving clinical acceptance and diagnostic accuracy unverified by experts.
The study did not incorporate patient metadata (e.g., age, gender, lesion location), which could improve classification accuracy and clinical relevance.
CNN and transformer-based models were implemented separately; the authors note that a hybrid architecture might enhance robustness, suggesting a limitation in the current architectural design.
The hardware used for training was limited (16 GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4050 GPU with 6 GB VRAM), which may constrain the scalability or complexity of models in resource-constrained settings.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; model comparisons rely on descriptive metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC) without significance testing.
The 'Indeterminate' class is very small (130 instances), raising concerns about the reliability of performance metrics for this specific class despite high AUC scores.
